retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Timely and coordinated consultation across specialties not only helps manage the initial event of NAION but also plays a pivotal role in preserving vision and systemic health moving forward. Please let me know if you'd like a summary table or a referral flowchart. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glaucoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the early signs and risk factors associated with glaucoma, including elevated intraocular pressure, optic nerve changes, and visual field defects. Screen patients at risk for glaucoma during routine eye examinations, including those with a family history, advanced age, diabetes, and other relevant medical conditions. Apply imaging modalities such as optical coherence tomography and visual field testing in glaucoma diagnosis, monitoring, and management. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare providers to provide comprehensive glaucoma care, ensure timely interventions, optimize outcomes, and prevent irreversible vision loss in patients with glaucoma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The endocrinologist sees difficult and complex patients and coordinates care with the PCP, ophthalmologist, podiatrist, and geneticist. Laboratory medicine advises on the appropriateness of tests, performs biochemical and serological tests, and communicates the results to the treating clinician. Bariatric surgeons must have a high index of suspicion for LADA in patients with diabetes and counsel them regarding the less-than-optimal glycemic control post-surgery. Healthcare professionals need coordinated efforts to achieve excellent glycemic control, prevent or delay complications, and substantially reduce morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs.

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Apoplexy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective interprofessional communication is central to patient safety and optimal outcomes in pituitary apoplexy. Rapid activation of multidisciplinary response pathways—linking emergency, endocrine, and neurosurgical teams—ensures that imaging, endocrine stabilization, and surgical decisions occur immediately. Structured handoffs, shared electronic documentation, and real-time updates facilitate seamless care transitions, while early involvement of ophthalmology guides visual prognosis and follow-up needs. By aligning expertise across disciplines, healthcare teams can minimize delays, reduce complication risks, improve visual and hormonal outcomes, and enhance patient confidence through clear, coordinated communication.

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon-Induced Retinopathy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The healthcare team should ensure regular screening for retinopathy from the beginning of treatment and at intervals throughout. Strict glycemic and blood pressure control is crucial for those with diabetes and hypertension. Patients need education on potential retinopathy symptoms and should know when to promptly report any visual changes. Interprofessional communication is vital among clinicians to ensure well-controlled comorbid illnesses and timely access to the latest laboratory evaluations and eye examination results. This coordinated effort significantly reduces the risk of vision loss associated with interferon-induced retinopathy and contributes to an overall reduction in morbidity and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Prognosis -- Factors influencing prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Timeliness of diagnosis and intervention: Early diagnosis and treatment significantly improve outcomes, particularly for patients with TVL caused by vascular conditions. Management of underlying conditions: Aggressive control of systemic diseases such as diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia is crucial. Patient compliance: Adherence to medical therapy, lifestyle modifications, and regular follow-up provider visits can improve the long-term prognosis. [95]

retrieved_docs.title: Nocardia Keratitis -- Consultations -- Key Consultation Workflows
retrieved_docs.content: When a smear reveals filamentous branching organisms, the ophthalmologist promptly contacts the microbiology laboratory to initiate Nocardia -specific culture protocols and notifies the pharmacy to prepare fortified amikacin. If deep stromal or scleral extension is present, or the patient is immunosuppressed, an infectious diseases consultation occurs within the first 24 hours to initiate systemic TMP-SMX and tailor dosing. Concurrently, internal medicine optimizes comorbid conditions, such as hyperglycemia, to support corneal healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Visual Acuity -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The change in frequency is to better assess for age-related ocular disease. Populations at higher risk for glaucoma should have comprehensive eye examinations performed more frequently, even when asymptomatic. Individuals diagnosed with type 1 diabetes should have an eye exam, including visual acuity, five years after diagnosis followed by yearly exams, while individuals diagnosed with type 2 diabetes should have yearly testing starting at the time of diagnosis. Additionally, women with diabetes should have a comprehensive eye exam, including visual acuity during the first trimester of pregnancy. The goal of these screening exams is to allow for early intervention to prevent long-term morbidity and enhance the quality of life. [3] [4] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: The Spherical Equivalent -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When patients are referred for spherical equivalent correction, clinicians must be on the lookout for signs suggesting underlying disease. In such cases, patients gain more quality care from a multifaceted assessment. The timely adoption of an interprofessional approach is critical for these individuals. People with diabetes with poor glucose control regimens, for example, may be first seen by the optometrist or ophthalmologist with complaints of transient blurry vision. These individuals may require multiple changes to spectacle prescription without timely referral to endocrinology for the underlying metabolic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should be counseled properly regarding the prognosis of diabetic retinopathy. Early diagnosis, management, and follow-up visits at timely intervals depending on the stage of presentation of diabetic retinopathy are mandatory. Systemic dysregulation of diabetes can lead to an exponential worsening of diabetic retinopathy. Lifestyle modification should be done along with proper systemic and topical medications to halt the progression of diabetic retinopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The American Academy of Ophthalmology's current recommendation states that people with diabetes mellitus type 1 should have yearly screening for diabetic retinopathy starting five years after the onset of diabetes. Patients with diabetes mellitus type 2 should have fundus evaluation at the time of diagnosis and annually  thereafter. [105] [106]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Treatment Planning -- Follow-Up Protocol
retrieved_docs.content: Initial follow-up: Weekly for 2 to 3 weeks to monitor disc edema and rule out progression. Systemic evaluation: Collaborative care with primary physicians, endocrinologists, and sleep specialists. Fellow eye surveillance: Patients should be educated on early symptoms of involvement. [99]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurotrophic Keratitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective collaboration within the healthcare team is essential for improving outcomes in neurotrophic keratitis management. Early screening and diagnosis require standardized corneal sensitivity testing in high-risk populations, including individuals with diabetes, prior HSV or VZV infection, or a history of neurosurgical procedures. A coordinated interprofessional approach, facilitated through regular team meetings among ophthalmology, neurology, and endocrinology specialists, helps tailor patient-specific treatment plans. Integrating electronic health records allows seamless access to patient history across specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: Fundus Camera -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The fundus camera is an instrument used for fundus photography. Fundus photography captures the images of the retina, optic nerve head, macula, retinal blood vessels, choroid, and the vitreous. Fundus photography impacts the detection and screening of various causes of treatable and preventable blindness, notably diabetic retinopathy, age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, and retinopathy of prematurity. Over the decades, the quality of fundus images and the ease of photography has improved significantly. This activity describes the techniques of fundus photography and the use of fundus cameras for the management of various ophthalmic conditions and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and managing the diseases of the vitreo-retina and optic nerve head with fundus photography.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The duration of diabetes is the most crucial risk factor for the development of diabetic retinopathy. In people with type 1 diabetes, it typically sets in about 5 years after disease onset. Hence it is recommended to start the yearly retinal exams in these patients about five years after diagnosis. Among patients with type 2 diabetes, many patients might already have retinal changes at the time of diagnosis. Approximately 10% at ten years, 40% at 15 years, and 60% at 20 years will have nonproliferative retinal disease. In these patients, the recommendation is to start the yearly retinal screening at the time of diagnosis. Study after study has shown that reasonable glycemic control favorably affected the onset and progression of diabetic retinopathy. Uncontrolled blood pressure is an added risk factor for macular edema. Lowering the blood pressure in patients with diabetes thus also affects the risk of progression of the retinopathy. Injection of antibodies vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) agents are generally in use as the initial therapy in cases of macular edema. In cases of nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy, pan-retinal photocoagulation is being used. In cases of diabetic proliferative retinopathy, combined modalities of anti-VEGF agents and pan-retinal photocoagulation are now in use. Sudden loss of vision can occur for several reasons in patients with diabetes mellitus, the most common being vitreous hemorrhage. Less common causes that merit consideration include vascular occlusion (central retinal vein or branch vein occlusion involving the macula), retinal detachment, end-stage glaucoma, and ischemic optic neuropathy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: داء ريفسام, indicating that ocular examination of the retina can reveal manifestations of this disease and underscoring the importance of retinal assessment for accurate diagnosis and management of patients suspected of having داء ريفسام. Understanding the relationships among these insertion procedures, retinal‑targeted radiation therapies, and the ocular and renal sequelae of secondary diabetes mellitus enables clinicians to select appropriate interventional techniques, monitor disease‑specific complications, and provide integrated care that addresses both procedural interventions and systemic effects, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1168
retrieved_docs.content: kidney disease. Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage kidney disease, underscoring the systemic impact of diabetes on both ocular and renal health. Collectively, these hierarchical relationships, anatomical links, procedural contexts, and clinical associations form an integrated framework that delineates the pathways from localized glomerular injury and metabolic disturbances to chronic and end‑stage renal disease, supporting comprehensive risk assessment and multidisciplinary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is identified as being mapped to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus), indicating that this combination of neurodevelopmental and metabolic abnormalities is considered a manifestation or subtype of diabetes. Similarly, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 is also mapped to مرض السكري, reflecting that transient neonatal diabetes is classified within the broader spectrum of diabetes mellitus. The hormonal contraceptive desogestrel and the medication ethynodioldiacetát are both contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, highlighting the need to avoid these drugs in diabetic individuals due to potential adverse effects or reduced safety. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, underscoring the well‑known relationship between diabetes and retinal complications such as diabetic retinopathy. Together, these connections emphasize that various neonatal and developmental forms of diabetes, specific pharmacologic contraindications, and retinal pathology are all interrelated aspects of the clinical picture of diabetes mellitus, informing both diagnosis and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1794
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) encompasses the specific condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is a recognized form of diabetes. Diabetes is clinically associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and more specifically with diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ). Both diabetes and diabetic retinopathy are also clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). These connections illustrate how diabetes can lead to multiple organ complications, linking retinal pathology and kidney failure, and emphasize the need for integrated monitoring and management of patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.
